speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. So you were saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as I was saying, this uniform man or countryman-- was he a soldier or a policeman-- he got me. And that's when I wet myself. He didn't know about it. And as we were walking towards the police station, I, I bargained with him and begged him to let me go, because I only helped another woman. And of course he saw the star on me. And I said, look, I am working in here. Just let me tell my auntie that I am going with you. He says, all right. Go in. I'll come with you. Because he was afraid that I shoot through. And my boss [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came out. He saw me with that uniformed person. And I told him what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went into a room with this policeman or whatever. And he said to me, go into, into the premises inside, into the factory. He must have given him some money, which was-- kept him quiet. He disappeared, the policeman, and I went back normal living. But I tell you what, it was an experiences I have. So what year was that? That was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother wasn't alive anymore. It wasn't '44 yet. Because in '44 winter, someway it was winter. I know I had my boots on. I know it was winter. I remember the people around me all winter clothing. But there wasn't snow. So it could've been March or April, or I can't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somehow that incident sort of sticks in your mind as--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: --also very much associated with the fact that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, star, yes. And I knew that I wasn't a Hungarian states person. So I knew that the minute they take me into the police station, they would shove me outside the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody's coming. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] after-- OK, so after we got to the point where you had returned back to-- back to your house with the relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was after the digging the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Labor camp. Labor camp, yes. So we lived there until September. In September there was a new law again, whoever is between 16 to 60 should be gathering here and there. And that's when my auntie was not let loose. She had to come with me. Now she arrived to this center with a high-heeled shoe on purpose. Because she thought if they would see her with the high-heeled shoes they're not going to make her march to the border town, but they did. So OK, sorry. Let's go back. You were asked-- this was in when, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September 1944, there was-- I don't know whether it was radio, or was it newspaper, or whatever. It was brought out an announcement that all Jews, women or men, who are between 16 to 60 are to be gathering together again in that sports ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same place where in March we had to gather together, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know why? Did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't. I can't remember whether they said why. I know that I had-- I know what I had on. I had a houndstooth coat on, which I inherited from my mother. I had a, a scarf, which I made a turban out of it on my head. It was getting cool, where it was in, I think, 20th of September or whenever it was. And I know that all the womens from that JOINT flat, where the Jewish flat where we already were, that means my auntie, my grandma, and my auntie's outside family. So her husband's parents, the old people, and the other sister-in-laws with their children. Now all the sister-in-laws had to gather down there as well in the sports ground. Because they were all between 16 and 60. And we all went to that sports ground. And they all had to come with me to the, the marching. And what they had done, we have marched first day, 25 kilometers to the brick factory of Obuda. It's a very well-known place by now. Because those people in Budapest, whoever were leftover, they all, every day, 2,500 had to gather together, and go to the brick factory, spend the night there under dreadful circumstances. And next day they gathered together the people, whoever survived it--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the people, you mean the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews who were gathered together on that sports ground. And then we, we walked again 25 kilometers to the next station. And that went on for five days. For every five days, we went 25 kilometers walking. Now, don't forget, by that time all those women, 90%-- there were hardly any men-- they were some sort of invalids in one way or other, men who were left behind. Because all of the others were already in force camp, labor camp. And there was one incident I want to mention in the brick factory, where one of the women-- I was sitting, laying down with my aunties, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] trying to discuss how are we going to try to run away or get away from that certain situation. When all of a sudden-- yeah-- and we, as the women in our family, had wedding ring or had a little [NON-ENGLISH] on the neck, and whatever. And of course, we were trying to hide it. Now what they've said, being sort of a young girl, they said they won't do anything to me. So they put all the jewelry into my turban. Because the turban looked like a scarf, which you twist around and then you put it into a roll. And into that roll, we had-- we hid all the jewelry, wedding rings, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were sort of made responsible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, we were all sitting together. But it was in mine, instead of somebody's pocket. It was in my turban. And one of the women in that other group, I mean, out of the 2 and 1/2 thousand women, went mental. And she was walking around with her hands out, and she was, altogether she had a mental breakdown like a zombie. And she was walking around, and constantly wanting to grab my turban. Well, we looked at each other, the aunties. And we thought that she might have seen how we put in the-- but she couldn't have. Because we did that underneath the coat, and covered it. So I don't know how or what, she wanted to have my turban. She was constantly walking around like a sleep-walker, you know, to grab it. Well, eventually we got her off that track. And I remember that another one was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had the diarrhea, and she used my coat to empty herself on. And of course that was the only warm thing I had. So whether there was something on it or not, I just brushed it off. We didn't have much water. But somehow I got rid of the smell, and we just-- and that coat was with me right through, luck or no luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those incidents with this woman and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was in that brick factory, while we were all either laying down or sitting up, and comforting each other in the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at that brick factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was all on that one night?

speaker: interviewee
text: All in that one night. And the next morning they took us again, gathered together on the street, and everybody was marching. Now by that time, a lot of them lost faith and strength, mostly the older ones and the sick ones. And as we were walking, by that time, we had some German soldiers or not Wehrmacht, but either SS or TOTs who were walking with us. And they had, of course, the guns. And whoever could not walk and sat down, they shot them on the highway. Now they shot them, and they covered them with some sort of paper, newspaper, brown paper, whatever they could find in the vicinity. Now at that time, I knew grandma had some gold coins. They were called Napoleon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, gave me gold coins. And I had a pair of-- I had two pants, one on top of each other. One was along within elastic on the bottom-- a rower, my uncle was a rower, professor rower, or hobby rower, or whatever. And he had sort of a drill material pair of trousers with elastic in the waist. And underneath that I had a culotte. And the culotte was stitched in the center, double that there was a little hole, a strip going up to my waist. And we stuffed the Napoleon in there. And Napoleon was like our, 6-- 5 cents now. But it was gold. And it was quite thick. Now that gold coin was quite valuable at those days. And people, they meant to kill for a Napoleon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Hungarian currency?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were French Napoleon money, which people somehow converted from the Hungarian money, which was not worth a pinch of salt, into Napoleon, because the gold was valuable. So I don't know how my grandma got them. Most probably that uncle who was well-to-do gave her some to survive, to try to rescue the children with that. And these were pushed up into my skirt. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I can't remember. It could have been five, eight, four. I can't recall. I only know that my auntie, in her high-heeled shoes, could not walk too far. And what these peasants, these country peasants-- because by that time we were already in the country area. We were out of Budapest. And there were some horse and carts country people who, for money, took the sick Jews from one station to another because they knew where the next transport comes to. Because these 2,500 per day went on for quite some time. Anyway, the-- my auntie got on one of them. And I had to take out one Napoleon and give it to the driver, to the horse and cart-- to the peasant. And that peasant dropped them off. He had about six people on his cart. And there were about four or five of these carts. And these people all had some sort of remuneration from these people who couldn't walk anymore. And every time we arrived to a station where we slept in the open area in September, whether it was a market, or marketplace or where they were selling animals, or a school yard or so on. Now grandma not only gave me the Napoleon, or my auntie at the time, but gave tea to make a cup of tea somewhere. The water was poisoned from Budapest to the border. The Germans and the Hungarians have poisoned it so that the Jews or the Russians will not be able to drink the water, what was in the wells or in the rivers. So automatically we couldn't make a cup of tea, because it was poisonous water. But I was hungry for a hot something in my stomach. And what I've done-- by that time I smoked cigarettes. The whole family did, grandma, my mother, my auntie, everybody. So I tore a piece of paper off the dead bodies, the tea leaves I put into there, rolled it, licked it, and smoked it. And that was for three months the only warm thing I had in my stomach. That was September, October, November. End of October, I think, I came back to Budapest. I can't recall exactly the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you mean that you were marching all that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not marching, going from-- well, it was a march. We went from one village, 25 kilometers away to the next, or another village. Where all these gathered together, and slept the night. And then in the morning we got up, and then we had to go another 25 kilometers. So by five days, we arrived to Garab, Gonyu. That is a quite a big city in, in Hungary. And then we got there. My auntie, who every day had to be on one of these carts, every day one Napoleon was gone as a payment for another peasant. And when I got to Gonyu, she wasn't there, only her luggage, her haversack. And I was a bit fearful of what happened to her. And then somebody said, look, she came with a peasant, with four people. And they didn't arrive, only their luggage. But they are all right. Don't worry. And somehow, I believed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after that, I find out that that peasant has hid these four women in his roof, in his-- not garage-- where the animals are kept, farm-- Barn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Barn. That's right. His barn roof, where all the straw was kept. And he hid them there until the next morning. The next morning when the train for, for Budapest arrived, he took them to the train, those four women. And they went back to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: But of course, at this time you did not know--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did know about any-- I only knew that she is not in the station where we arrived at, and that she's all right, so I was told. Now I took her haversack. And I thought to myself, well if she's not here, I am going to try to get back as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at this stage, were you completely on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely on my own. By that stage all my other aunties already somehow or other disappeared from the scene. I think the other two aunties who were with us from-- in the, in the brick factory, I think from the brick factory they somehow went back. How it happened, I can't remember. I only know that they did not march with me anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just to clarify something else, Lilly, when you were on the march, I mean, you mentioned that you didn't have anything to drink in terms of the water. What was there to eat and drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every station they have waited for us with a huge big, barrel, or whatever you want to call it, with soup, which they have put in roots of carrots or potatoes, and that's what they have given out to all these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But by the time it came to me, or towards the end or the middle of the-- of the 2,500, by that time it was only 2,000 because quite a few died on the way, or they shot or so on and so forth. There was-- I remember we ate something. But I can't-- I only know that there was-- what-- oh, how can I translate it? You know what the pigs eat when you have all the food, the remnants put together in a-- in a-- Like a compost?

speaker: interviewee
text: A compost sort of thing, it was dreadful. So we did eat something. But I can't think. I know that we sometimes when nobody was watching, we took out from the earth, carrots, and ate it raw as it was. So it gave us a bit of vitamins and whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So eventually when we arrived in Gonyu, and my auntie wasn't there, I started and tried to take my trip back. Now funny enough, on that station they announced it that anyone who can prove that they are either sick or they can't walk anymore or they are between 16 and 60 again-- or they, who are under 16 and over 60, they should come to that part of standing on the side. And the other was the normal 16 to 60 should be here. And then they take them out over the border, most probably to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew where they went to, because I haven't met any of them coming back. And that was the time when I tried to take off. So what I've done, I said I am under 16. There was a German soldier, SS, standing-- it was a big yard, with a well in the middle, where the well had a bucket to pull out some water from it. And the SS was standing on the side, I remember without a hat. And I remember it was already October. It was like October, end of October, beginning of November. And he was standing there in a very casual way, with his riding boots still here. I'll never forget his face. And as we were lining up, and he was trying to do a Mengele-- you to the right, you to the left. And I said, I am under 16. He said, you're not 16. You're over 17, which I was. I looked older. I had a scarf on my head. No, I didn't. I didn't have a scarf at that stage. And then when he put me over to the ones to go out, I grabbed there. I went over to a girl, and said, look, try to change clothing with me because he recognizes me if I try again to go into that way. It was the circumstances were such that I could have gone again into that line, but she gave me her scarf. I gave her my coat. And she gave me her coat. And with that, I went into the line. And I got through.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he think you were under 16?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He thought then under 16. And he got me through. He didn't recognize me, because I changed clothing. And that was when we marched back to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And again, it took us five days to get back. We walked. That was already somehow-- we had more confidence we were on the way back. So the spirit was better. When I arrived in Budapest, they took us into the ghetto. And we arrived in the ghetto to the-- to the Jewish welfare department. And I was looking for my brother. I knew that he must be here somewhere. And eventually I found him. So we shacked up together. My auntie and grandma, I found as well. They just a day before were gone out from the ghetto, and went into a Swedish protected house. Now it's a well-known fact that Wallenberg came to Hungary and bought from the Nazis certain buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was-- who was Wallenberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wallenberg was a Swedish diplomat who came from neutral Sweden, and wanted to save as many Jews as possible. And he paid Swedish money for every Jew he could save, and for all the buildings which he bought from the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were these buildings within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, outside. The ghetto was a different kettle of fish. That was all those Jews who couldn't afford-- and you see, the ghetto was wired to be exploded. It never came to it. It never got to that stage. But it was wired, so the Germans thought that last minute-- they knew they lost the war. But they thought and they hoped that they just push a button, and the whole ghetto will go into air. It didn't come to it. But Wallenberg bought houses outside the ghetto, which was more an area, an area where everybody was living sort of. And he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came to Hungary, to Budapest. Well, I came back, as I said, into Budapest. And my brother was in one of the apartments. My grandma and my auntie was already out in a protected Wallenberg house, a Swedish house. I, I don't remember seeing them. I think I met them once before I had to go into the ghetto permanently.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the ghetto in Budapest, was it like--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Jewish area, a very Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it enclosed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed-- were they-- were there physical boundaries to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, physical boundaries and guards at the gates. So it was made out of wooden, wood pieces, and a gate door, a wooden gate door with a handle on it. And the guide on the outside. Something happened when I was with my brother there, coming back from that trip, we were very hungry, both of us. Fresh bread, we didn't know what it was. But I have somehow found an arcade. Would you believe going through to the ghetto, coming out in the non-ghetto area.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we took off our yellow star, my brother and I, from our coat, and walked into that arcade, and queued up outside the ghetto at a bakery shop, where all the Christians were. Well, of course, we were afraid. The star was not on us. But the mark was there. Because it leaves-- it gets dirty around it. And it leaves a bleach mark, because we had to wear that right through all summer, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we stood there. We did that twice that we stood. My brother bagged it. He just couldn't cope with the fright. [LAUGHS] I wasn't very brave either. Because if they would have caught us, they would have shot us on the spot. Anyway, so we queued up, we took the bread. We took it back. And we ate and ate until we got green in the face. As I said, we did that once more. And then we, we just couldn't. Then they closed-- somebody found out about this arcade, and they closed the gate. So they were smart. I worked, because I didn't want to stay in that ghetto room. We were about 17 people in one room of this size. Everybody was sleeping on the floor. There were families who were in the ghetto. And Freddy and I were on the floor as well. I can't remember. I think it was a mattress, a very low mattress, where we were laying down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any of the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were always amongst strangers. The only people I knew was grandma and auntie. And they were not in the ghetto anymore, with the little Vera. And they were living outside the ghetto. At that time I didn't know what's going to happen with them or with us. We thought-- we knew that the Russians are on the border. We knew that it can't last for long. We were only hoping that we'll survive it, until the end comes. Now December came. Christmas came, 24th. And the day before, Wallenberg came to the ghetto and had discussions with the Jewish official people. But they didn't speak German. And because my German was school German and good German, I was translating between Wallenberg and the Jews. And as a matter of fact, there was a woman with him whose name was Asta Nielsen. Asta Nielsen is a very well-known-- or was a very well-known actress in Sweden, like Ingrid Bergman. And she came, accompanied Wallenberg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I translated. And Asta Nielsen gave me her card when we were in the ghetto. And they were there as guests. They didn't stay in the ghetto. But on Christmas day-- night, 24th, Wallenberg has gotten permission that he takes out 12-- no, 23 Jews from the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: We might just stop there.
